title: Macroprudential Policy with Liquidity Panics
abstract: We study the optimality of macroprudential policies in an environment where banks provide liquidity to firms. Informational frictions between banks can cause interbank market freezes, prompting firms to accumulate their own liquid assets. Liquidity hoarding by firms in turn reduces the demand for bank loans and bank profitability, makes interbank market freezes even more likely, and may ultimately trigger a self-fulfilling bad equilibrium. Such ‚Äúliquidity panics‚Äù provide an additional rationale for liquidity requirements on banks, which alleviate frictions in the banking sector and, paradoxically, can increase aggregate investment. Instead, policies encouraging bank lending can have the opposite effect.

author.Alonso Villacorta*: University of California, Santa Cruz, USA

article.1. Model:
JEL G01 - Financial Crises G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages G28 - Government Policy and Regulation Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

A common view in the macroprudential policy debate is that policies such as liquidity or capital requirements imply a trade-off between stability and growth. Such requirements are seen as a buffer that must be accumulated in good times, at the expense of lower investment levels, to eventually cope with financial crises by limiting bank losses or agency frictions.

We highlight a new mechanism that indicates that this trade-off may not necessarily be present once the reaction of the corporate sector is taken into account. Policies restricting bank lending, such as liquidity or reserve requirements, despite mandating that banks store part of their balance sheets in low-yield assets, can actually end up increasing aggregate productive investment, even in times of financial stability. Conversely, policies encouraging lending in crises, such as subsidies to the banking sector, can make it harder for the economy to recover from a financial crisis.

To provide intuition for these results, let us outline the basic elements of our theoretical three-period model. Consider an environment where banks finance the corporate sector‚Äôs liquidity needs, such as short-term investment opportunities, as inKashyap, Rajan, and Stein (2002). Under normal conditions, banks channel their resources to firms so they can seize investment opportunities. However, banks, which are heterogeneous in their lending efficiency, are subject to informational frictions, as inBoissay, Collard, and Smets (2016). First, banks‚Äô lending efficiency is private information. Second, there is moral hazard, as banks‚Äô shareholders or managers can strategically default on their creditors. Bank heterogeneity gives rise to an interbank market where funds flow from less to more efficient banks, which ultimately lend to firms. Yet, informational frictions might disrupt this market. When bank profitability is too low, interbank trade is not incentive compatible and the interbank market freezes.1This is socially suboptimal because low-efficiency banks store their assets in an unproductive technology instead of lending to high-efficiency banks that would channel the funds to firms.

Given the possibility of an interbank market freeze and the associated reduction in bank lending, firms may react by accumulating more liquid assets to be able to self-finance their liquidity needs. Importantly, this reduces the demand for bank loans, which lowers bank profitability and tightens banks‚Äô incentive compatibility constraint, making it more probable that an interbank market freeze will take place. A more probable freeze in turn reinforces firms‚Äô incentives to accumulate liquid assets, triggering a feedback loop that we call a ‚Äúliquidity panic.‚Äù

The liquidity panic feedback can generate multiple equilibria since greater liquidity accumulation is rational when the probability of a liquidity shortage is higher. Moreover, for a given equilibrium selection criterion, it can also lead to amplification of shocks: a small change in parameter values can induce liquidity hoarding by firms and make good (i.e., low-hoarding) equilibria unattainable, leading to a nonlinear drop in aggregate investment and welfare. Indeed, even under the assumption that agents can coordinate on the best available equilibrium, we show that a social planner would choose a lower level of firm liquidity holdings than in the decentralized equilibrium, as it internalizes the strategic complementarities between firms. We keep this equilibrium selection assumption for the main policy analysis in order to focus on the amplification effect of liquidity panics rather than on equilibrium coordination issues, but show that the main channels would remain if instead we randomized across all available equilibria.

In the presence of liquidity panics, common macroprudential policies can affect aggregate investment and welfare in opposite directions. Policies that oblige banks to set aside a fraction of their portfolio in liquid assets, such as liquidity or reserve requirements, can relieve the agency problems between banks and prevent liquidity panics. These policies limit the gains from defaulting and relax the conditions for a trade equilibrium in the interbank market, diminishing the probability of a freeze and thus mitigating liquid asset accumulation by firms. But a reduction in firm liquidity accumulation in turn increases the demand for bank loans and bank profitability, which lowers the probability of a market freeze further. As a result, a restrictive policy can lead to lower inefficient storage, higher aggregate productive investment, and higher welfare in a nonlinear manner. Of note, this policy is only beneficial collectively due to its general equilibrium effects‚Äîit is never individually optimal for banks to borrow and store resources.

On the contrary, policies encouraging bank lending, such as subsidies to bank financing, do not help correct liquidity panics. Such policies may marginally alleviate firm constraints in the midst of a crisis, as they tend to expand bank lending to firms. However, in equilibrium they reduce bank profitability, making it harder to satisfy the conditions for a trade equilibrium and thus to prevent a liquidity panic. We show that subsidies to bank financing unambiguously reduce welfare if they are funded with lump-sum transfers. A negative interest rate on reserves would also marginally increase the supply of loans during banking crises, and, although it does not increase the probability of liquidity panics, it does not correct them either.

The study of self-fulfilling panics in financial markets was pioneered by the bank-run model ofDiamond and Dybvig (1983)and extended byCooper and Ross (1998),Goldstein and Pauzner (2005), andUhlig (2010), among others. A more recent literature shows how frictions in the interbank market can lead to run-like market disruptions.Diamond and Rajan (2011)provide a multiple-equilibrium model where the prospect of a fire sale by impaired banks can incentivize healthy banks to accumulate cash, causing a credit freeze. Closer to our setup,Malherbe (2014),Heider, Hoerova, and Holthausen (2015), andBolton, Santos, and Scheinkman (2021)develop models where asymmetric information about bank asset quality and liquidity needs leads to adverse selection, as buyers of bank assets do not know why a particular bank is selling. Under such frictions, the fear of future illiquidity may spur banks to hoard liquid assets at present and lead to equilibria with limited trade.

In these models with asymmetric information, a crucial determinant of an interbank market breakdown is the return on bank assets‚Äîexcessively low returns impede the market from solving the disruptions generated by informational problems. None of the above papers focuses on how bankborrowersreact to a breakdown and potentially affect the probability of a breakdown itself. Instead, we take a general equilibrium approach and endogenize the impact of the response of bank borrowers on the return on bank assets. Specifically, we show that firms accumulating their own liquidity to protect against a banking crisis erode bank returns, making a crisis more likely.

Moreover, the theoretical literature considers runs on only one side of banks‚Äô balance sheets. The papers above feature liability-side runs either by depositors (Diamond and Dybvig 1983) or through a freeze in the market for bank financing (Malherbe 2014;Heider, Hoerova, and Holthausen 2015;Bolton, Santos, and Scheinkman 2021), whereas a recent example of asset-side runs isInfante and Vardoulakis (2021), a model in which bank borrowers can run to withdraw their collateral. From this perspective, another novelty of our model is that it generates endogenous runs on both assets and liabilities simultaneously, and highlights the interplay between the two. An interbank market freeze can be interpreted as a run of bank creditors (liability side), whereas cash accumulation by firms can be interpreted as a run by bank borrowers (asset side). Indeed, the empirical literature shows such double runs were a key feature of both the Global Financial and COVID Crises (see, e.g.,Acharya and Mora 2015andAcharya, Engle III, and Steffen 2021, respectively).

A complementary strand of the literature studies the macroeconomic effects of banking crises, but again does not consider the endogenous feedback from the corporate liquidity margin to the banking sector. Key contributions includeGertler, Kiyotaki, and Prestipino (2016)andKashyap, Tsomocos, and Vardoulakis (2014), who embed bank runs in a general equilibrium model, andBoissay, Collard, and Smets (2016), whose model of interbank frictions we borrow in this paper.

As regards the literature on macroprudential policy and banking, it has mainly focused on the moral hazard problem generated by implicit government subsidies to bank debt-holders (Kashyap, Rajan, and Stein 2008;Admati et al. 2010;Begenau 2020). Nevertheless, our model is in line with the view inFrench et al. (2010)that a central problem during the financial crisis was that disruptions in the financial system‚Äôs ability to intermediate were the source of panics for other agents in the economy.Freixas, Martin, and Skeie (2011)discuss optimal monetary policy in a banking-sector model with liquidity runs, but without considering the response of the corporate sector.
We develop a three-period(t=0,1,2)model with endogenous banking crises and a liquidity accumulation decision by firms. The model provides a framework to evaluate macroprudential policy under the possibility that liquidity panics take place.

2.3 Welfare

2.5 The liquidity panic feedback

An increase inCaffects the marginal return to cashŒ¶(C)in two ways (see Equations (10)‚Äì(11)). On the one hand, it reduces the demand for loans and lowers the corporate loan rate both during normal timesRBN(A,C)and during banking crisesRBF(A,C)‚Å†. This direct effect lowers the marginal return to cash and is captured by the partial derivativeŒ¶^C(C,A‚Äï)<0‚Å†. On the other hand, an increase inCraises the probability of a banking crisisG(A‚Äï)‚Å†. This is because the measure of states in which a banking crisis occurs expands with the level of cash holdings, that is,A‚Äï‚Ä≤(C)>0(see Proposition 2). For these additional states where a banking crisis appears due to a marginal increase inC‚Å†, the corporate loan rate jumps up, making it more valuable to accumulate cash. Thus, this indirect effect increases the marginal return to cash and is captured byŒ¶^A‚Äï(C,A‚Äï)A‚Äï‚Ä≤(C)‚Å†, whereŒ¶^A‚Äï(C,A‚Äï)‚â°[RBF(A‚Äï,C)‚àíRBN(A‚Äï,C)]dG(A‚Äï)dA‚Äï>0whendG(A‚Äï)dA‚Äï>0‚Å†.

This indirect effect is behind the central mechanism in the paper: theliquidity panicfeedback. When the firm expects a greater probability of a banking crisis, which is associated with a contraction in the supply of loans and higher loan rates, it reacts by increasing its cash holdings. The feedback then arises because the increase in aggregate cash holdings raises the thresholdA‚Äï‚Å†, implying an even greater probability of a crisis, which further increases the incentives to accumulate cash.

The liquidity panic feedback affects the equilibrium whenC‚àófeaturesG(A‚Äï(C‚àó))>G(A‚Äï(C=0))‚Å†, that is, when the equilibrium response of firm cash accumulation expands the number of states in which a banking crisis takes place.8

Next, we show how the liquidity panic feedback can both be a source of equilibrium multiplicity and generate amplification of shocks.

Equilibrium multiplicity.Given the two opposing effects described above, the total marginal effect of aggregate cash accumulation on the return to cashŒ¶(C)can be positive or negative, that is,Œ¶(C)can be nonmonotonic. If this is the case, then condition (12) could be satisfied at different values ofC‚Å†, implying multiple equilibria. The existence of multiple equilibria requires that the rateRKcuts the functionŒ¶(C)at the range where it is nonmonotonic.

Models in the theoretical literature typically feature runs on only one side of banks‚Äô balance sheets (e.g.,Diamond and Dybvig 1983;Goldstein and Pauzner 2005). Instead, the liquidity panic feedback in our model endogenously generates simultaneous runs on both assets and liabilities. An interbank market freeze can be interpreted as a run of bank creditors (liability side), whereas we can think of cash accumulation by the firm as a run by bank borrowers (asset side) when it causes a nonlinear drop in the demand for bank loans. As described in Section5, the empirical literature shows that such double runs were a key feature of the Global Financial and COVID Crises.

Amplification mechanism.Henceforth, we assume the existence of a coordination device that allows agents to choose the best possible equilibrium. We use such a benevolent equilibrium selection criterion to highlight the amplification of shocks due to the liquidity panic feedback, rather than coordination failures.9For example, inFigure 4,C‚àóis also affected by the liquidity panic feedback. It features excessive cash holdings because if the firm had not reacted (‚Å†C=0‚Å†), a banking crisis would not have happened in stateAM‚Å†. Thus, our model does not require sunspot shocks to generate nonlinear amplification from financial crises.

Moreover, this method for selecting equilibria has the desirable feature that market freezes (or runs) do not occur at random times. Instead, their probability depends on the state of the economy.10To illustrate how liquidity panics can be an amplifier rather than a coordination failure that triggers crises, we present comparative statics to an increase in the probability of a low technological realization, which can be interpreted as bad news on real economic developments. In this example, if the firm does not hold cash, a banking crisis occurs only in the low state:A‚Äï(C=0)‚àà(AL,AM)‚Å†. WhenpLis sufficiently low, this also happens for the case of the equilibrium levelC‚àósince the probability of a banking crisis is too low to require the firm to accumulate high levels of cash and induce a liquidity panic. AspLincreases, though, the firm tends to accumulate more cash, eventually reaching the point where a liquidity panic appears. From that point onward, a banking crises occurs also whenA=AM‚Å†, creating even more incentives for the firm to accumulate cash.

The liquidity panic leads to a jump in expected storage in the economy as the fraction of banks lending their resources falls during a banking crisis. Since storage constitutes an inefficient allocation of resources and a reduction in aggregate output, welfare drops (see Equation (9)). Absent a liquidity panic, allowing for cash accumulation is beneficial for the economy, as it permits the firm to increase its investment in the interim project. However, when a liquidity panic occurs, the feedback mechanism makes cash accumulation detrimental.

article.4. Macroprudential Policy:
We proceed to analyze the optimality of different macroprudential policies that have recently been implemented by central banks. We deliberately abstract from important determinants of optimal macroprudential policy, such as implicit bailout guarantees, to concentrate on the new mechanism in this paper: liquidity panics. Our goal is to highlight the additional effects that each policy has on the aggregate economic variables once the amplification loop between interbank market freezes and liquidity accumulation by bank borrowers is taken into account. We consider two policy interventions: liquidity requirements and subsidies to bank financing. AppendixA.1considers an alternative specification of the liquidity requirement and AppendixA.2shows the impact of negative interest rates on reserves.

4.1 Liquidity requirement

The ‚ÄúLiquidity Coverage Ratio‚Äù rule introduced by Basel III requires a bank to hold sufficient high-quality liquid assets to cover its potential net cash outflow over 30 days. High-quality liquid assets are cash or assets that can be immediately converted into cash at no loss, such as central bank reserves.

A common view in the macroprudential policy debate is that liquidity requirements imply a trade-off between stability and growth or output levels (see, e.g.,Boissay and Collard 2016). Such requirements are seen as a buffer that must be accumulated in good times at the expense of limiting banks in their role of financing the real sector and supporting investment. We show that this trade-off does not necessarily arise. Once one considers the feedback effects from the corporate sector‚Äôs reaction to liquidity crises, liquidity requirements can have a positive effect on investment, even in normal times. Guaranteeing stability in the financial sector can correct liquidity panics and substitute excessive cash hoardings with productive investment.

In our model, the liquidity requirement mandates that banks keep in storage a fractionœàof the fundsŒ¥they borrow from other banks, obtaining returnRS‚Å†. AppendixA.1shows that this specification (in line with Basel III) is preferable to a liquidity requirement proportional to total bank liabilities. We assume that the assets stored for regulatory compliance are observable and cannot be diverted upon default. An interpretation is that they take the form of reserves at the central bank.15

The liquidity requirement thus restricts the actions of borrower banks at t = 1 ‚Å† . They must keep a fraction œà Œ¥ per unit of initial funding in storage/reserves and can only lend ( 1 + Œ¥ ( 1 ‚àí œà ) ) to the firm. article.5. Empirical Motivation:
This section reviews the empirical literature and evidence of the two-way relation between liquidity accumulation and financial crises.

Corporate liquidity hoarding.The corporate finance literature has extensively documented that firms react to worsened financial conditions by increasing their liquidity holdings.

Almeida, Campello, and Weisbach (2004)show that financially constrained firms have a higher propensity to save cash out of cash inflows than unconstrained firms. They interpret this to mean that these firms accumulate cash in anticipation of potential constraints and liquidity needs in the future.Acharya, Almeida, and Campello (2007)find that financially constrained firms with high hedging needs display a strong preference for saving cash rather than paying out debt in order to hedge for states of the world where investment opportunities arise and external finance is costly.Acharya, Almeida, and Campello (2013)provide evidence that firms that are more sensitive to banking industry downturns are more likely to hold cash.

Bates, Kahle, and Stulz (2009)andDenis and Sibilkov (2010)analyze the secular increase in cash holdings of U.S. firms since the 1980s.Bates, Kahle, and Stulz (2009)conclude that the main reason for this trend is a stronger precautionary motive for cash accumulation due to changes in firm characteristics: cash flows have become riskier, R&D investment is now more important, and firms hold less inventory.Denis and Sibilkov (2010)show that higher cash holdings allow financially constrained firms to undertake value-increasing investment projects that are otherwise bypassed.

Double bank runs.Our theoretical result that there is also a causal relation in the other direction, that is, that higher levels of liquidity holdings may worsen financial conditions, is based on a general equilibrium effect, so is harder to directly test with firm- and bank-level data. One key implication, though, is that banks experience simultaneous runs on both sides of their balance sheets.

While this is a novel feature of our model theoretically, the empirical literature has found evidence that banks experienced sizable double runs in recent crises.Ippolito et al. (2016)show that during the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) banks more affected by runs of their creditors (liability side) also experienced more draw-downs of their credit lines to firms (asset side).Acharya and Mora (2015)describe the onset of the GFC as a ‚Äúcrisis of banks as liquidity providers‚Äù to firms, and document how the real economy tried to bypass the ailing banking sector by accumulating cash, as occurs in our model. Finally,Acharya, Engle III, and Steffen (2021)describe a similar pattern during the COVID-19 crisis, when credit-line runs affected bank valuations as an independent source of risk, and argue that macroprudential policy should account for such asset-side liquidity risk.

The Global Financial Crisis and the liquidity panic.Next, we provide evidence that the evolution of the key aggregate variables in the aftermath of the GFC was consistent with the model‚Äôs predictions, although of course no causality can be attributed. Both the United States and the Euro Area (EA) experienced a collapse in the interbank loans market (Figure 12), followed by a large contraction in credit to nonfinancial corporations (Figure 13) and a spike in liquidity hoarding by nonfinancial corporations (Figure 14).

Figure 12plots the annual growth in interbank loans divided by GDP. For the United States, the variable is the outstanding amount, while for the EA the available variable only includes new issuances of cross-border liabilities.19Thus, the two series are not entirely comparable, but they both show that banks significantly reduced their borrowing from other banks after the onset of the GFC. In our model, this fall is captured as an interbank market freeze.

Figure 13shows the growth rate in credit to nonfinancial corporations over GDP. After having experienced a boom before the crisis, this variable tanked in both economies, with the decline in the EA lagging the United States by 1 year.20

While the previous two facts are widely documented, the increase in aggregate liquidity accumulation has received relatively less attention.21Figure 14plots growth in liquid assets over total assets of all nonfinancial corporations.22As the supply of credit to firms was being reduced, firms started accumulating more liquid assets. In fact, they seem to have done that after setting a low record in 2007, just before the GFC.

To be clear, our interpretation is not that the accumulation of cash by firms was the ultimate cause of the GFC. The initial trigger may have been a deterioration in expectations about either the return on bank assets, the return on investment opportunities, the intensity of moral hazard, and/or more generally the overall state of the economy. However, such exogenous shock(s) may have unleashed a liquidity panic that ended up amplifying the recession. For example, as shown inFigure 5, in the model an exogenous increase in the probability of the low statepLcan trigger a liquidity panic in additional states of the world, which ends up amplifying the welfare loss.
article.6. Conclusion:
This paper considers a theoretical setup where banks act as liquidity providers to firms, but are prone to endogenous crises due to informational frictions in the interbank market. Moreover, firms have the option of accumulating liquidity internally to insure against liquidity shortages from the financial sector. When these two elements are combined, the possibility of liquidity panics emerges. Firms accumulate more liquidity in response to financial crises, and this response in turn exacerbates those crises. Liquidity panics provide an additional source of equilibrium multiplicity and, for a fixed equilibrium selection criterion, they lead to nonlinear amplification of aggregate shocks.

Given the presence of liquidity panics, it is important that the analysis of macroprudential policy assesses not only the direct effects on the banking sector, but also the response by the corporate sector. This additional decision margin leads to the paradoxical result that policies restricting lending can end up increasing aggregate investment even in times of financial stability, whereas policies encouraging lending can at best act as a marginal relief in crises. Liquidity panics thus provide an additional reason for stricter regulation of banks‚Äô balance sheet positions.

The liquidity panic feedback is not circumscribed to our particular model of the banking sector. We model banking crises as a freeze in the interbank market for tractability and because we think this provides a relevant description of the events around the Global Financial Crisis. However, a similar feedback loop would arise in any multiple-equilibrium model with the following two fairly general features. First, runs on banks‚Äô liabilities occur when profitability or returns on bank intermediation are low. Second, on the asset side, bank borrowers react to the possibility of banking crises by hoarding cash and further depressing bank returns endogenously.

The literature has developed alternative models with the first feature above. For example,Goldstein and Pauzner (2005)demonstrate that adding noisy signals to multiple-equilibrium games, such as theDiamond and Dybvig (1983)bank run model, leads to a unique equilibrium in which fundamentals, related to the return on bank assets, determine the probability of a bank run. Similarly, inBebchuk and Goldstein (2011), the probability of a credit market freeze depends precisely on the expected profitability of bank loans. Extending such frameworks with the second feature‚Äîan asset-side reaction that further decreases bank profitability during crises‚Äîwould result in a similar feedback mechanism.

B. Equilibrium Selection Criterion

Section2.4showed that the liquidity panic feedback can lead to multiple equilibria, which required the specification of an equilibrium selection criterion to proceed with the policy analysis.

In the main text, we assumed that agents were able to coordinate on the best possible equilibrium. Here we consider a more general criterion: a random sunspot shock induces agents to coordinate on either of the possible equilibria. We then calculate welfare as the unconditional expectation of the welfare function given the probability of the sunspot shocks.
